records.ety: [AS. arm, earm; akin to OHG. aram, G., D., Dan., & Sw. arm, Icel. armr, Goth. arms, L. armus arm, shoulder, and prob. to Gr. <?/ joining, joint, shoulder, fr. the root <?/ to join, to fit together; cf. Slav. rame. <?/. See Art, Article.]

defs: The limb of the human body which extends from the shoulder to the hand; also, the corresponding limb of a monkey.
The fore limb of an animal, as of a bear.
A limb, or locomotive or prehensile organ, of an invertebrate animal.
A slender part of an instrument or machine, projecting from a trunk, axis, or fulcrum; as, the arm of a steelyard.
The end of a yard; also, the part of an anchor which ends in the fluke.
An inlet of water from the sea.
A support for the elbow, at the side of a chair, the end of a sofa, etc.
Fig.: Power; might; strength; support; as, the secular arm; the arm of the law.
quotes: To whom is the arm of the Lord revealed?

defs: A branch of the military service; as, the cavalry arm was made efficient.
A weapon of offense or defense; an instrument of warfare; -- commonly in the pl.

records.ety: [OE. armen, F. armer, fr. L. armare, fr. arma, pl., arms. See arms.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Armed (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Arming.]

defs: To take by the arm; to take up in one's arms.
quotes: And make him with our pikes and partisans A grave: come, arm him.
Arm your prize; I know you will not lose him.

defs: To furnish with arms or limbs.
quotes: His shoulders broad and strong, Armed long and round.

defs: To furnish or equip with weapons of offense or defense; as, to arm soldiers; to arm the country.
To cover or furnish with a plate, or with whatever will add strength, force, security, or efficiency; as, to arm the hit of a sword; to arm a hook in angling.
Fig.: To furnish with means of defense; to prepare for resistance; to fortify, in a moral sense.
quotes: Arm yourselves . . . with the same mind.

defs: To provide one's self with arms, weapons, or means of attack or resistance; to take arms.
